summary: The hypothalamus controls the anterior pituitary via releasing and inhibiting hormones through the portal system.
The hypothalamus directly secretes hormones into the systemic circulation.
The anterior pituitary controls the hypothalamus via feedback hormones.
Thyroid hormones T3 and T4 require iodine for synthesis and are derived from tyrosine residues.
Thyroid hormones are derived from tryptophan residues on thyroglobulin.
Thyroid hormones T3 and T4 require selenium for their synthesis.
In Graves' disease, thyroid-stimulating antibodies activate TSH receptors causing hyperthyroidism.
In Graves' disease, thyroid-blocking antibodies cause hypothyroidism.
Graves' disease is caused by iodine deficiency leading to goitre.
Insulin is secreted by beta cells and promotes glucose uptake by peripheral tissues.
Insulin is secreted by alpha cells and promotes glycogenolysis.
Insulin is secreted by delta cells and inhibits glucagon release.
Cortisol is secreted by the zona fasciculata and regulated by the HPA axis.
Cortisol is secreted by the zona glomerulosa and regulated by the renin-angiotensin system.
Cortisol is secreted by the adrenal medulla under sympathetic stimulation.
Aldosterone is secreted by the zona glomerulosa and regulates sodium and potassium balance.
Aldosterone is secreted by the zona reticularis and regulates calcium metabolism.
Aldosterone is secreted by the zona fasciculata alongside cortisol.
Growth hormone release is stimulated by GHRH and inhibited by somatostatin.
Growth hormone release is stimulated by somatostatin and inhibited by GHRH.
Growth hormone is released continuously without hypothalamic regulation.

overallFeedback.feedback: Review endocrine physiology and try again.
